summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirmed that a customer mailbox on alpha.local can be authorized to send as finance@beta.local, even though beta.local belongs to a different customer.
expected_security_impact: The proof should show that customer alice can add finance@beta.local as an allowed sender for alice@alpha.local and that Froxlor's sender-auth query then resolves finance@beta.local to alice@alpha.local.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh cloned Froxlor tag 2.3.4, built the reproduction image, initialized a fresh Froxlor database, and executed the real EmailSender.add path as customer alice. The run reported pre-exploit sasl_users=<empty> for finance@beta.local and post-exploit sasl_users=alice@alpha.local after the alias insertion.
verification_method: The helper script creates two customers and two local domains through Froxlor's own APIs, creates alice@alpha.local, invokes EmailSender.add with allowed_sender=finance@beta.local as customer alice, verifies the inserted row in mail_sender_aliases, and then executes the same SQL shape Froxlor ships in its Postfix sender-auth configuration to confirm that the victim-domain sender resolves to the attacker's mailbox only after exploitation.
evidence_produced_by_run: Console output showed [evohunt] post-exploit sasl_users: alice@alpha.local.
Console output showed inserted_alias={"email":"alice@alpha.local","allowed_sender":"finance@beta.local"} in the proof details payload.
Console output ended with EVOHUNT_SUCCESS: alice@alpha.local became authorized to send as finance@beta.local, even though beta.local is owned by customer bob.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable ref, and mounts that checkout into the container.
